title: Support for signing and verification of "docker-archive" tarball file
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind feature

**Description**

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

Would like to check if podman supports signing and verifying "docker-archive" tarball file and if not, any way to do that? Due to some reason, we cannot use container registry and have to export container image as docker-archive tarball and move them around. We are following https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/docs/tutorials/image_signing.md, however, seems it only supports podman push --sign-by <gpg key email> image:tag to image registry, once I use "podman push --sign-by <gpg key email> image:tag docker-archive:/tmp/image", it throws ""Cannot determine canonical Docker reference for destination docker-archive:/tmp/image". seems related to https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/main/vendor/github.com/containers/image/v5/copy/sign.go#L58, if we don't use image registry, is there any way to support image signing and verification and what is your suggested approach? Thank you.


**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**
3.4.2

comments.body:
It’s impossible, the `docker-archive` format can’t carry the information required for the current signature concept. Besides not having a way to record signatures, it doesn’t have a manifest to sign; there just isn’t any way to transfer a bit-by-bit identical copy of an image using the docker-archive transport, and the bit-by-bit identical copy is what would need to exist for it to be signed.

You can use the `dir` transport to transfer a single image. (Short-term, within somewhat close versions of tools; it’s not really designed as a long-term archival format).

For anything larger-scale I recommend:
- on the source, temporarily run a registry container, with the backing storage mounted into the container as a volume
- do a registry-to-registry copy of the images to that temporary registry
- stop the registry, and archive the storage volume contents, along with lookaside-pending (sigstore-pending) directory structure containing the signature
- transfer that archive around
- on the recipient, temporarily run a registry container, with the backing storage mounted into the container as a volume
- do a registry-to-registry copy of the images from that temporary registry to the final destination
- publish the lookside-pending data on the lookaside web server.

Using a temporary registry this way allows benefiting from the existing cross-image data sharing, and operation parallelism, implemented for registry accesses. And it’s going to be _quite noticeably_ faster than anything involving `docker-archive`.

Thank you, @mtrmac for your suggestion. Would like to check if you see issue or risk in the following process. Thank you
1. Use podman save to docker-archive/tarball file
2. Generate hash1 for the docker-archive/tarball file (e.g. with md5sum)
3. Before podman load docker-archive/tarball file on target machine, compute hash2 and compare with the original hash1 to check docker-archive/tarball file has changed.

Well, SHA256 instead of broken MD5.

Sure, that’s viable _for what it does_: it doesn’t allow maintaining an end-to-end signature from the original image producer across such a transport mechanism.
